proof_stages.label: Crystalline symmetries and orientation quotients
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up crystals as ordered structures with finite rotational point-group symmetries, explains polycrystalline grains, and models grain orientations as rotations modulo the crystal point group.

proof_stages.label: Grain-boundary parameters and the coloring obstruction
proof_stages.summary: A grain boundary is reduced to macroscopic parameters consisting of a misorientation and a boundary normal, and the immediate goal is to continuously and injectively encode the three-dimensional misorientation space in three-dimensional color space, despite the nontrivial topology of SO(3).

proof_stages.label: Misorientation equivalences and two-dimensional motivation
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the equivalence relations coming from independent point-group choices in the two grains and from grain exchange, with the SO(2) case showing how quotienting by inversion can reduce topological complexity.

proof_stages.label: Fundamental zones for orientation spaces
proof_stages.summary: Using quaternion fundamental zones and their boundary identifications, the orientation spaces SO(3)/G are identified as lens spaces or more general spherical 3-manifolds, all retaining embedding dimension at least five and hence not solving the color-space problem.

proof_stages.label: No-symmetry misorientation quotient
proof_stages.summary: For the trivial point group C1, grain exchange identifies each quaternion representative with its inverse, yielding a cone on RP^2 and making the quotient simply connected.

proof_stages.label: First-six point groups reduce to the cone case
proof_stages.summary: The C2 fundamental-zone calculation shows how mirror-type boundary identifications deform the rotational-symmetry misorientation quotient back to SO(3)-type topology, so after grain exchange the first six point groups have the same cone-over-RP^2 topology as C1.

proof_stages.label: Last-five point groups yield a colorable three-ball
proof_stages.summary: For the octahedral-type cases, the mirror identifications fold the rotational-symmetry quotient into two 3-balls glued to form S^3, and grain exchange cuts this to a 3-ball, enabling the desired continuous one-to-one coloring of misorientations.

proof_stages.label: Extension to the complete five-parameter boundary space
proof_stages.summary: The final part adds the boundary-normal sphere, rotational and grain-exchange actions, and the no-boundary singularity, then partially simplifies the full five-parameter space by reparameterizing normals and viewing it through suspension models.
